metadata.summary: Significance testing for A/B tests: raw-count templates, power analysis, worked examples, and automated Python helper.

content.title: Statistical Significance: Application
content.summary: Practical tools for applying significance testing: raw-count templates, power analysis template, three worked examples (significant result, insufficient data, underpowered test), and Python helpers. Requires raw counts (n1, x1, n2, x2) — never run from headline percentages alone.

why.p: Growth teams waste traffic by running underpowered tests and make wrong decisions by analyzing results before the pre-registered sample size is reached. Standardized templates force pre-registration of hypothesis, primary metric, and sample size before launch — preventing post-hoc metric selection and peeking.

list.item: Analyzing a finished A/B test and needing a defensible significance and CI verdict.
Sizing an experiment up-front (power analysis) so you do not waste traffic.
Suspecting an "underpowered win" and needing to compute actual achieved power.
Standardizing stat reporting across many small tests so results are comparable over time.
Causal inference from observational data (no random assignment) — significance tests over-claim.
Multi-armed or sequential or bandit experiments — z-tests inflate false positives; use SPRT, group sequential, or Bayesian.
Fewer than 30 events per variant — use Fisher's exact test.
Highly skewed continuous metrics (revenue, session length) — use bootstrapping or t-test on log-transformed data.

section.name: worked-examples
section.p: Three A/B Test Analysis Examples: significant result, insufficient data (needs more), and underpowered test.

example.title: Example 1: Significant Result — Ship
example.code:
Test: New checkout button color (gray → green)
Data: A (gray): n1=15,000, x1=450 → p1=3.00% B (green): n2=15,000, x2=525 → p2=3.50%
Calculation: p_pooled = 975/30000 = 0.0325 SE = sqrt(0.0325 * 0.9675 * (1/15000 + 1/15000)) = 0.00129 z = (0.035 - 0.030) / 0.00129 = 3.88 p-value = 0.0001
Result: p < 0.05 → Statistically significant 95% CI: [+0.25%, +0.75%] — zero not in interval Decision: SHIP green button

example.title: Example 2: Not Significant — Need More Data
example.code:
Test: Headline copy change
Data: A: n1=2,000, x1=100 → p1=5.0% B: n2=2,000, x2=110 → p2=5.5%
Calculation: p_pooled = 0.0525 SE = 0.00158 z = 0.317 p-value = 0.75
Result: p > 0.05 → NOT significant 95% CI: [-2.6%, +3.6%] — interval includes zero Decision: Need ~10,000 per variant to detect this 0.5% absolute difference
example.note: Do not report as "no effect". It means "insufficient evidence". The true lift could be anywhere in the CI range.

example.title: Example 3: Underpowered Test — Useless Result
example.code:
Setup: Baseline: 10%, MDE: 5% relative (0.5% absolute) Actual sample: 500 per variant
Required sample calculation: n = 16 * 0.10 * 0.90 / 0.005^2 = 57,600 per variant
Actual power: ~6% (only 6% chance of detecting the real effect)
Conclusion: This test was essentially useless. Even if B is genuinely better, there was a 94% chance of missing it with this sample.
example.note: Running this test wasted 1,000 user-exposures and produced an untrustworthy null result. Always compute required sample size before launch.

rule.statement: Always require raw counts (n1, x1, n2, x2) before computing significance; refuse to calculate from headline percentages alone.
rule.rationale: LLMs confidently compute p-values from percentages without knowing sample sizes, producing meaningless results. Raw counts are the only defensible input.

rule.statement: Pre-register primary metric, sample size, and end date BEFORE launch; refuse to analyze a test where these were not fixed in advance.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc metric selection and peeking invalidate p-values. Tests without pre-registration are exploratory at best — report them as such.

rule.statement: Always report 95% CI alongside p-value; output a binary significant_at_0.05 field rather than reporting "almost significant" (p=0.06).
rule.rationale: "Almost significant" is not a statistical concept. p=0.06 is not significant at alpha=0.05. CI gives the range of plausible effects — report both.

rule.statement: Apply Bonferroni correction (alpha/k) when checking more than 3 metrics; designate one primary metric and treat the rest as guardrails.
rule.rationale: With 10 independent metrics at alpha=0.05, expected false positives = 0.5 per test. Bonferroni correction divides alpha by the number of comparisons.

section.name: limitations-and-gotchas
section.p: Two-proportion z-test assumes independence; clustered users (multiple sessions) violate this and inflate significance. Peeking and early stopping silently breaks p-values; agents must refuse to compute on incomplete tests unless explicitly told it is a sequential design. Multiple comparisons (10 metrics, 5 segments) need Bonferroni or FDR; default scipy.stats.proportions_ztest does not correct. Frequentist p-values get mistaken for "probability B is better"; agents should report Bayesian posterior or CI alongside. MDE relative-vs-absolute confusion is the #1 source of wrong sample sizes. Asymmetric traffic split (90/10) breaks the simplified formula; use statsmodels or evan-miller calculator instead. When test has 0 conversions in one variant, z-test breaks; fall back to Fisher's exact and tell the agent to detect this case.

list.item: Asymmetric traffic split (90/10): use statsmodels, not the simplified formula
Revenue or AOV metric: use bootstrapping (10k resamples), not proportions z-test
0 conversions in one variant: use Fisher's exact test; z-test breaks
Multi-armed or sequential design: use SPRT or Bayesian — standard z-test inflates FPR

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A sonnet analyst agent ingests raw counts (n1, x1, n2, x2), runs the test, and emits a structured verdict (p, CI, power, decision). For sizing, it accepts (baseline, MDE, alpha, power) and returns sample-per-variant and duration estimate. An opus reviewer agent sanity-checks the test design before launch (correct primary metric, no peeking plan, no proxy metric). A haiku agent can pull counts daily for monitoring but must NOT report significance until pre-registered sample size is hit.

Recommended Subagents: faion-growth-agent (sonnet for analysis, opus for design review), generic stats subagent with scipy and statsmodels access, review subagent (opus) for pre-launch hypothesis and metric audit.

CLI Tools: Python scipy.stats with proportions_ztest, chi2_contingency, fisher_exact. Statsmodels for power analysis with NormalIndPower().solve_power(). R pwr package for reference power calcs. Evan Miller calculators as web UI sanity-check at https://www.evanmiller.org/ab-testing/. Abracadabra (Square OSS) for Bayesian and frequentist A/B in Python. Expan (Zalando OSS) for experimentation analysis.

Services and Apps: Statsig (SaaS, REST and SDK) for sequential testing and CUPED variance reduction. Eppo (SaaS, REST) for warehouse-native stats engine. GrowthBook (OSS, REST and self-host) with Bayesian by default. Optimizely Stats Engine (SaaS) with sequential testing built-in. VWO SmartStats (SaaS) Bayesian, agent reads results via API.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Pre-register the primary metric, sample size, and end date BEFORE launch; agents should refuse to analyze tests where these were not fixed. Always report CI alongside p-value; "significant" with CI [0.01%, 4%] is barely actionable. Convert MDE to absolute terms in the prompt explicitly to dodge the relative-absolute trap. Use one-sided tests only when a regression would never be shipped — otherwise default to two-sided. For revenue and AOV use bootstrapping (10k resamples) — proportions math is wrong. Apply Bonferroni when checking more than 3 metrics; better, pick one primary and treat the rest as guardrails.

antipattern.description: LLMs confidently compute p-values from headline percentages without raw counts.
antipattern.reason: Always force n1, x1, n2, x2 as explicit inputs; percentages without sample sizes are meaningless.
No, it is not. Force a binary significant_at_alpha field; there is no "almost significant" in frequentist stats.

antipattern.description: "95% CI does not cross 0" is misinterpreted as "95% chance B is better".
antipattern.reason: That is Bayesian. Frequentist: "If H0 is true, there is a 5% chance of seeing this data." Report CI: "We are 95% confident the true lift is between X% and Y%."

antipattern.description: Sample-size prompts drop alpha and power assumptions silently.
antipattern.reason: Sample size is meaningless without alpha and power. Require them in output: alpha=0.05, power=0.80 (or whatever was used).

template.purpose: Template for recording raw data, z-test, CI, and decision.
Template for pre-test sample size calculation with MDE notation.
